name: Peña Oroel Ascent from Jaca
description: Hike Peña Oroel from Jaca, a difficult 10.9-mile trail with 3112 feet of elevation gain and panoramic Pyrenees views.
region_intro: Hiking trails around Jaca are set within the Aragonese Pyrenees, offering a diverse landscape of mid-mountain ecosystems, dense forests, and striking conglomerate escarpments. The region features picturesque valleys like the Natural Park of the Western Valleys and is marked by iconic peaks such as Peña Oroel. Hikers can explore a variety of terrains, from lush woodlands to high-mountain lakes and unique geological formations. Best hiking trails around Jaca The most popular hiking route is Tour of the Ayous Lakes, a 11.7 miles (18.9 km) trail that takes 7 hours 50 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores a series of high-mountain lakes amidst the Pyrenean landscape. Another top favourite among local hikers is Peña Oroel Ascent from Jaca, a difficult 10.9 miles (17.6 km) path. This challenging ascent offers panoramic views across the Aragon valley from the iconic Peña Oroel summit. Local hikers also love the PR-HU 81 Jaca, Rapitán, and Ipás Trail, a 3.7 miles (6.0 km) trail leading through mid-mountain terrain with views of the Fuerte del Rapitán, often completed in about 2 hours 9 minutes. Hiking around Jaca is defined by Pyrenean valleys, dense forests, and iconic mountain peaks. The network offers options for easy walks, moderate excursions, and more challenging alpine ascents. The routes in Jaca are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 600 reviews. More than 6000 hikers have used komoot to explore Jaca's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Jaca?
A: Jaca offers a wide variety of hiking experiences, with over 120 trails documented on komoot. These routes cater to all levels, from easy walks to challenging alpine ascents, exploring the diverse landscapes of the Aragonese Pyrenees.

Q: Are there easy hiking trails suitable for families in Jaca?
A: Yes, Jaca has numerous family-friendly options. There are over 40 easy routes, perfect for a leisurely day out. These trails often wind through lush woodlands and picturesque valleys, providing accessible paths for all ages. While specific family-friendly routes aren't highlighted in the data, the region's diverse terrain ensures many gentle options.

Q: What kind of natural features and landmarks can I expect to see while hiking near Jaca?
A: The Jaca region is rich in natural beauty and historical landmarks. You can explore stunning high-mountain lakes, known as &lt;em&gt;ibones&lt;/em&gt;, such as those found on the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e937788066/tour-of-the-ayous-lakes" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tour of the Ayous Lakes&lt;/a&gt;. Iconic peaks like Peña Oroel offer panoramic views, and you might encounter unique geological formations like the Foz de Biniés. Additionally, cultural sites like the Monastery of San Juan de la Peña are integrated into the hiking landscape. For specific viewpoints, consider visiting &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2839913" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Oroel Summit and Cross&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/307857" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lac Gentau and View of Pic du Midi d'Ossau&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes around Jaca?
A: Yes, there are several circular routes available. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/19885720" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parador de Oroel Viewpoint – Peña Oroel Summit and Cross loop from Peña Oroel / Peña Uruel&lt;/a&gt; offers a moderate 7 km circular hike with significant elevation gain, providing rewarding views of the iconic Peña Oroel.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Jaca?
A: Jaca offers hiking opportunities throughout much of the year. Spring and autumn are ideal for pleasant temperatures and vibrant landscapes. Summer is excellent for higher altitude routes, though some lower trails can be warm. Winter hiking is possible, especially on lower-altitude trails, but higher mountain areas may require snowshoes or specific winter gear due to snow and ice conditions.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about the trails in Jaca?
A: The hiking trails around Jaca are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average rating of 4.5 stars from over 600 reviews. Hikers frequently praise the region's diverse terrain, from dense forests to high-mountain lakes, and the stunning panoramic views from peaks like Peña Oroel. The well-maintained paths and the integration of natural beauty with historical sites are also often highlighted.

Q: Are there any challenging or advanced hiking routes in the Jaca area?
A: Absolutely. For experienced hikers seeking a challenge, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e932602609/pena-oroel-ascent-from-jaca" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Oroel Ascent from Jaca&lt;/a&gt; provide a difficult 17.6 km path with significant elevation gain, culminating in extensive views from the summit. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e937788066/tour-of-the-ayous-lakes" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tour of the Ayous Lakes&lt;/a&gt;, an 18.9 km difficult trail exploring a series of high-mountain lakes.

Q: Is it possible to hike with dogs on trails near Jaca?
A: Many trails in the Jaca region are dog-friendly, allowing you to explore the Pyrenean landscapes with your canine companion. However, it's always advisable to check specific regulations for protected natural areas like the Natural Park of the Western Valleys or Ordesa and Monte Perdido National Park, as rules regarding dogs may vary. Always keep your dog under control and clean up after them.

Q: What are the parking options for hikers accessing trails around Jaca?
A: Parking is generally available at trailheads and in nearby villages. For routes starting directly from Jaca, there are town parking facilities. For trails further afield, such as those leading to Peña Oroel or into the valleys, designated parking areas are usually provided. It's recommended to arrive early during peak season to secure a spot.

Q: Can I reach hiking trailheads near Jaca using public transport?
A: While Jaca itself is accessible by public transport, reaching specific remote trailheads directly by bus can be limited. Some popular starting points, like those for Peña Oroel, might have more direct connections or be within a reasonable taxi ride. For exploring the broader Pyrenean valleys, a car often offers more flexibility. Always check local bus schedules for the most up-to-date information on routes from Jaca to surrounding areas.

Q: Are there any permits required for hiking in the Jaca area or nearby natural parks?
A: For most general hiking trails around Jaca, permits are not typically required. However, if you plan to venture into specific protected areas like Ordesa and Monte Perdido National Park or the Sierra de Guara Natural Park, there may be regulations regarding access, especially during peak seasons or for certain activities. It's always best to check the official websites of these parks for any permit requirements before your visit.

Q: Are there any trails that offer views of historical sites or cultural landmarks?
A: Yes, hiking in Jaca often blends natural beauty with cultural exploration. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e946128228/pr-hu-81-jaca,rapitan,and-ipas-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;PR-HU 81 Jaca, Rapitán, and Ipás Trail&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, offers views of the Fuerte del Rapitán. Additionally, the region provides access to the Monastery of San Juan de la Peña, a spectacular Romanesque building nestled within a protected landscape, which can be integrated into hiking itineraries.
tips:
It is difficult to find a person from Jaca who has never climbed the Oroel rock.
The silhouette of this mountain presides over the Aragón valley and stars in all the postcards of the city.
At its top, at 1769 meters high, there is a large iron cross of 10.4 meters. It was built in 1901 with the aim of being visible from Jaca and was paid for with donations from the residents themselves.

In the parador de la Peña Oroel there is a fresh water fountain, an area with picnic areas under trees and a very complete panoramic view of Jaca with the Pyrenees in the background.
Currently the restaurant is closed, but some summers it opens and serves typical food from the Aragonese Pyrenees, mainly migas.
On good weather days, it is common to see many families having picnics in the area, as there is a children's playground next to the picnic areas.
It is also the most popular point from which to face the climb to Peña Oroel. From here the famous 33 curves to the cross begin.

Special place inside the forest on the way up to the Oroel parador.
The wooden walkway marks the beginning of the section of the route that runs parallel to a small stream and is the greenest and most leafy area.

faqs.question: Where does the trail to Peña Oroel start, and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The trail typically starts from the Parador de Oroel, which is approximately 9 km from Jaca. This starting point is easily accessible by car, and parking is available there.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the hike to Peña Oroel?
faqs.answer: The hike to Peña Oroel is generally rated as moderate. While accessible to many, it involves a significant elevation gain of around 948 meters. Good fitness is recommended, and sturdy hiking shoes are advised, especially for some steep and rocky sections like the "33 Bends of Oroel."

faqs.question: What can I see along the route to Peña Oroel's summit?
faqs.answer: The route offers diverse scenery. You'll pass through a dense forest of pines, firs, and beeches, and as you gain elevation, the views open up. Notable points include the Parador de Oroel Viewpoint, and a Wooden footbridge. The highlight is undoubtedly the Peña Oroel Summit and Cross, offering magnificent 360-degree panoramic views of the Aragonese Pyrenees and the city of Jaca.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike Peña Oroel?
faqs.answer: The trail is enjoyable for much of the year. Spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures and beautiful foliage. During winter, the trail can be hiked, but be prepared for snow and potentially icy conditions, which may require snowshoes or crampons.

faqs.question: Is the Peña Oroel trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, the trail is generally dog-friendly. However, as it is located within the Protected Landscape of San Juan de la Peña and Monte Oroel, it's always best to keep your dog on a leash to protect local wildlife and ensure a pleasant experience for all hikers.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for hiking Peña Oroel, or specific regulations to be aware of?
faqs.answer: No specific permits or entrance fees are required to hike Peña Oroel. However, the area is part of the Protected Landscape of San Juan de la Peña and Monte Oroel. Please respect the natural environment by staying on marked paths, not disturbing wildlife, and carrying out all your trash.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route to Peña Oroel intersects with or runs concurrently with sections of several other paths, including "Subida a Oroel," "Camino San Salvador," and "Camino a la Cruz de Oroel." You may also encounter parts of "Ermita de la Cueva" and "Sendero río Gas."

faqs.question: What kind of views can I expect from the summit?
faqs.answer: From the summit of Peña Oroel, you'll be treated to exceptional 360-degree views. These encompass a vast expanse of the Aragonese Pyrenees, including prominent peaks like Orhi, Aneto, Peña Forca, Bisaurín, and Monte Perdido, as well as the Aragón valley and the city of Jaca itself.

faqs.question: Is the trail suitable for families with children?
faqs.answer: While some sources suggest it can be family-friendly, the trail involves a significant elevation gain and some steep, rocky sections. It might be too strenuous for very young children or those unaccustomed to hiking. Good fitness and sturdy shoes are recommended for all participants.

faqs.question: What are some notable historical or natural features along the way?
faqs.answer: Beyond the stunning views, the trail passes by historical features such as old snow pits ("Collado de las Neveras") and offers glimpses of the hermitage of the Virgin of the Cave. The area is also rich in biodiversity, and you might spot birds like the bearded vulture and golden eagle.

faqs.question: What should I pack for the hike?
faqs.answer: Given the trail's moderate difficulty and varied terrain, we recommend packing sturdy hiking shoes, sufficient water, snacks, sun protection (hat, sunscreen), and layers of clothing as weather conditions can change, especially at higher elevations. A map or GPS device is also advisable.
